title: The Presidial System
subtitle: Garrisons and artillery of Spanish and Mexican California, 1769–1846, against the Russian benchmark
abstract: Every documented count of men and cannon at the California presidios, drawn from the muster rolls and ordnance inventories in the C-A transcripts. Open any fort for its full dated series with citations. The point the numbers make: in 1813 a single Russian outpost at Ross reported about 13 mounted cannon, while the entire San Francisco defense had about 5 serviceable guns at its documented best (1802). Province-wide in 1830 there were 54 pieces on paper, most unserviceable, on gun platforms too rotten to work. Where two Savage copies of the same report disagree, both figures are shown.
legend_note: Province-wide snapshots: 403 men and $98,650 situado (1799, C-A 10/16); 432 men (1827) and 420 (1828, C-A 56); 54 cannon total across all four presidios (1830, C-A 18 Doc 278); ~64–74 part-time militia gunners for the whole province (1816–19).

features.name: Presidio of San Francisco
features.summary: The northernmost presidio, founded 1776, responsible for the whole contested frontier facing Bodega and Ross. Its company ran about 30 to 56 men for most of the Spanish period and never surged to meet the Russian arrival in 1812.
series: 36 men, each with escopeta, sword, lance, cuera, adarga
56 soldiers + 2 artillerymen at the fort (Bouchard year)
features.result: Roughly 30 to 70 men guarding the Golden Gate across five decades

sources.citation: Presidio garrison & artillery register (vault), compiled from C-A 10, 16, 21, 54

features.name: Castillo de San Joaquín (Fort Point, San Francisco)
features.summary: The battery at the Golden Gate narrows, blessed December 1794. Built for 6,491 pesos, partly with mission mule labor. The register's headline series: the fort's serviceable guns never exceeded a handful, and a single Russian outpost at Ross reported more mounted cannon than the whole San Francisco defense.
series: 1 cannon at Cantil Blanco, and it burst
6 cannon, all unserviceable (Arrillaga); the C-A 15 copy of the same report says 2 — copy discrepancy, flagged
3 iron 24-pdr + 1 iron 12-pdr in the batteries + 8 bronze 8-pdr half-culverins
~13 pieces but only ~5 mounted and serviceable
24-pdrs rusted, few serviceable bronze guns, buildings threatening collapse
15 cannon counted (likely all pieces incl. dismounted — reconcile before citing as mounted)
8 cannon, 2 broken, 6 functional; no forge, no grapeshot
the only port with heavy guns: 2 (or 3) cannon of caliber 24 — Savage copies disagree
features.result: At its documented best (1802): about 5 mounted serviceable guns

sources.citation: Presidio garrison & artillery register (vault); build cost C-A 24 Docs 62, 70

features.notes: The 1793 (2 vs 6) and 1823 (2 vs 3) figures each differ between Savage copies of the same report; both discrepancies are preserved rather than harmonized.

features.name: Batería de Yerba Buena (San Francisco)
features.summary: The cove battery east of the presidio, garrisoned in the 1790s by a Catalan Volunteers detail relieved every 8 days. Ordered recomposed with adobe, earth, and fascine in 1799; abandoned by 1800 per the intelligence layer of the borderlands map.
series: alférez + 1 cabo of Catalan Volunteers on rotation

features.name: Presidio of Monterey
features.summary: The capital's presidio and the largest company in the province, with the Real Cuerpo de Artillería headquartered here. Even so, in 1809 its ~85 men were dispersed as mission escorts and herd guards, with only 4 men on the battery.
series: ~85 men but dispersed; only 4 on the battery
82–85 troops monthly; 2 cannon worked by militia gunner J. J. Vallejo during Bouchard's attack
cannon unusable for want of vent-pins
features.result: The largest single garrison, comparable to Ross's combined personnel, 100+ miles from the contested ground

features.name: Presidio of Santa Bárbara
features.summary: Founded 1782 with about 58 men and holding near that strength for decades. In 1797 its artillery inventory recorded 118 cartridges and no artilleryman.
series: 58 men (founding year; Sgt. Sal among them)
57 soldiers; artillery inventory: 118 cartridges, no artilleryman
presidio guns: 1 bronze 6-pdr + 1 iron 3-pdr + 1 of caliber 1
1 bronze 6-pdr + iron 4-pdr + small pieces, several without carriages
~66 men (Capt. J. Argüello; Tte. de la Guerra)
bronze 2 good / 1 bad / 1 useless; iron 1 good / 2 useless

features.name: Presidio of San Diego
features.summary: The first presidio, founded 1769. In September 1783 the whole company held 72 cartridges. Its guns were dismounted in 1793 with nobody able to operate them.
series: 50 men; 35 pairs pistols, 1 sword, 72 cartridges for the company
2–3 cannon, dismounted, 'sin haber quien los maneje'

features.name: Fort Guijarros (Punta de Guijarros, San Diego)
features.summary: The harbor battery on Ballast Point, formally the Castillo de San Joaquín de Punta de Guijarros. The province had two forts of that name; this is the San Diego one. In 1798 it mounted 7 six-pounders of which 2 were serviceable; the 1820 census found the bronze guns vent-worn and the carriages rotted.
series: shot for 12/8/6/4/2-pounders + 15 bronze 4-pdr balls in store
'Cinco de los cañones de bronce se hallan muy desfogonados; las cureñas no sirven'

features.name: Presidio of Loreto (Baja California)
features.summary: The old Baja capital, included for context: its company (47 to 85 men plus a small marine department) was comparable to any Alta California presidio.

features.name: Colony Ross (Russian benchmark)
features.summary: The measuring stick. A single Russian-American Company outpost, founded September 1812 with about 95 Russians and 80 Alaska Native hunters. A 1813 Spanish intelligence report credited it with about 13 mounted cannon; Moraga's 1814 eyewitness reconnaissance counted 8 cannon in two corner towers with embrasures for far more. Either figure matches or exceeds the serviceable artillery of the entire San Francisco defense in the same decade.
series: ~95 Russians + ~80 Aleut hunters; stockade ~150 yards square
~13 mounted cannon (Spanish intelligence estimate, Viceroy Calleja)
8 cannon in two corner towers + ~26 embrasures; 'not easy to attack' (Moraga, eyewitness)
features.result: One Russian fort ≈ the principal Spanish fort, in guns and in men
features.notes: Both the Ross figure (a Spanish estimate) and the SF figure (a Spanish self-inventory) are Spanish-sourced, so the comparison is internally consistent. The defensible claim is order-of-magnitude parity, not a precise gun-for-gun ratio.

features.name: Mission San Buenaventura (armed)
features.summary: Armed against the coastal threat: 2 four-pounders in the 1798 census, 3-pounders by 1805.

features.name: Mission Santa Bárbara (armed)
features.summary: One 2-pounder in the 1798 census of the armed Channel missions.

features.name: Mission La Purísima (armed)
features.summary: One 2-pounder in 1798, still armed in the 1803 and 1805 censuses.

features.name: Mission Santa Inés (armed)
features.summary: 3-pounders in the 1805 census of armed Channel missions.

features.name: Mission San Fernando (armed)
features.summary: 2-pounders in the 1805 census of armed missions.

features.name: Mission San Juan Bautista (armed, post-Bouchard)
features.summary: Up to 3 cannons emplaced in 1819 'para impedir el desembarco de algunos invasores', part of the scramble after Bouchard burned Monterey.

features.name: Pueblo San José (pedrero)
features.summary: A pedrero (swivel gun) posted at the pueblo in the 1798 ordnance census.

features.name: Mission San Luis Obispo (pedrero)
features.summary: A pedrero listed at 'Mission San Luis' in the 1798 Monterey-district ordnance census.
features.notes: The 1798 census says 'Mission San Luis' without specifying Obispo or Rey; Obispo is the Monterey-district reading. Flagged.
